title: Oat scones
subtitle: Scottish-style scones made with rolled oats and plain flour
excerpt: Scones made with half rolled oats and half plain flour, giving them a heartier, nuttier flavour than a plain scone and a more open, slightly crumbly crumb. A Scottish tea-room standard, good with butter and sharp marmalade. Less sweet than a cream tea scone and considerably more satisfying at breakfast.
sourceNotes: Oat scones are part of the Scottish baking tradition that uses oats extensively across savoury and sweet baking. The combination of oats and wheat flour in a scone dough is documented in Scottish domestic cookbooks of the twentieth century and is a natural extension of the use of oatmeal in griddle breads and bannocks. The recipe here synthesises the established Scottish domestic formula for oat scones.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully baked scones once cool. Thaw at room temperature for 1 hour or warm in a low oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best within 2 hours of baking. Freeze rather than storing overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 200°C. Oat scones take 1 to 2 minutes longer than plain flour scones because the oats absorb moisture more slowly. They are done when the top is deep gold, the sides have opened, and the base sounds hollow when tapped.

glossaryTerms.definition: An oat scone has a more open, slightly rougher crumb than a plain flour scone because rolled oats do not form gluten and do not bind in the same way as flour. The oats also absorb liquid more slowly, which means oat scone dough may feel slightly drier or rougher than a plain scone dough immediately after mixing — this is normal. Leave the mixed dough to rest for 2 minutes before pressing out, which gives the oats time to absorb the liquid and makes the dough easier to handle.

content.text: is heartier and more open than a plain scone. The dough feels slightly rougher after mixing — leave it to rest for 2 minutes before pressing out, and it becomes more manageable as the oats hydrate. The method is otherwise identical to a plain scone.

Heat the oven to 220°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Mix the flour, oats, baking powder, salt, and sugar in a large bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Beat together the milk and egg, then pour into the dry ingredients and mix with a knife until the dough just comes together. Leave to rest for 2 minutes.

Turn onto a lightly floured surface and press to 2.5 cm thickness. Cut into rounds of 5 to 6 cm, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the lined tray, brush the tops with milk, and bake for 14 to 16 minutes until deep gold on top, the sides have opened, and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Cool briefly on the tray before eating.
